title: Sideband fingerprints of antibunched light in cascaded quantum wave mixing
authors: R. D. Ivanovskikh, W. V. Pogosov, A. A. Elistratov, A. Yu. Dmitriev, T. R. Sabirov, A. V. Vasenin, S. A. Gunin, O. V. Astafiev
abstract: Quantum wave mixing on a single superconducting qubit produces a hierarchy of coherent side peaks associated with elastic multiphoton scattering pathways. In a cascaded source--probe geometry these pathways become sensitive to the photon statistics of the radiation emitted by the source qubit. We develop an analytical theory of this effect starting from the cascaded master equation in the weak-driving regime. In the coherent-filtering limit $γ_{\rm s}\ggγ_{\rm pr}$, the standard coherent--coherent wave-mixing hierarchy is recovered. In the opposite limit $γ_{\rm pr}\ggγ_{\rm s}$, side peaks associated with multiphoton absorption from the antibunched source field are parametrically suppressed. Numerical solutions confirm the analytical scaling laws. The resulting sideband hierarchy provides a frequency-domain fingerprint of antibunched itinerant microwave light.
summary: This paper develops a theory showing that the sideband peaks in quantum wave mixing can act as a fingerprint for antibunched light. It demonstrates that by tuning the linewidth ratio of a source and probe qubit, one can observe the parametric suppression of specific multiphoton pathways. This allows for the frequency-domain identification of the photon statistics of microwave radiation.
main_problem: Developing an analytical theory to use cascaded quantum wave mixing (QWM) as a spectroscopic tool to identify the photon statistics (specifically antibunching) of incident microwave radiation.
main_result: The study establishes that the sideband hierarchy in QWM provides a frequency-domain fingerprint of antibunched light, where higher-order sidebands are parametrically suppressed based on the linewidth ratio of the source and probe.
method: The authors use a weak-drive Taylor expansion and a Neumann series expansion of the stationary cascaded master equation, validated by numerical solutions of the full dynamics.
model_or_system: A cascaded waveguide-QED system consisting of a source qubit and a probe qubit coupled unidirectionally to a one-dimensional microwave waveguide, modeled using a cascaded master equation.
key_observables: Probe coherence, probe inversion, and the amplitudes/frequencies of the QWM sideband hierarchy.
important_parameters: The ratio of decay rates (gamma_s/gamma_pr), the dimensionless cascaded coupling (alpha), and the drive amplitudes (Omega_s, Omega_pr).
main_assumptions: The theory assumes a weak-drive regime, a stationary (steady-state) approximation, and negligible back-action from the probe to the source.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of multiphoton processes; Figure 2 compares analytical and numerical QWM spectra in the antibunching and coherent-filtering regimes; Figure 3 illustrates the scaling of side-peak amplitudes as a function of the source linewidth.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of using QWM for photon statistics, presents the analytical framework using a master equation and Taylor expansion, discusses the two physical limits (coherent-filtering vs. antibunching), provides numerical validation, and concludes with the diagnostic utility of the sideband hierarchy.
why_interesting: It provides a novel spectroscopic method to characterize the quantum nature of itinerant light using nonlinear wave mixing, which is highly relevant for characterizing photon statistics in circuit QED and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic representation of a multiphoton process generating the −3𝛿𝜔side peak. Blue arrows indicate absorption of photons at the probe drive frequency 𝜔pr. Green arrows correspond to the source frequency 𝜔s. The red arrow denotes emission at the mixed frequency 2𝜔pr −𝜔s, which corresponds to the −3𝛿𝜔component in the rotating frame.

Figure 2: (a) QWM spectrum in the antibunching regime. The analytical result (red solid line) is compared with the numerical solution of the stationary cascaded equations (blue dashed line). (b) The same comparison in the coherent-filtering regime. (c) Analytical comparison of the antibunching spectrum (blue solid line) and the coherent-filtering spectrum (green dash-dotted line). In both cases 𝛾pr = 5, 𝜀pr = 0.2, and 𝜀s = 0.1. In the coherent-filtering case 𝛾s = 25, whereas in the antibunching case 𝛾s = 1.

Figure 3: Dependence of the normalized side-peak amplitudes on the source linewidth: (a) +3𝛿𝜔, (b) −5𝛿𝜔, and (c) +5𝛿𝜔. Red solid lines show numerical results obtained from the stationary cascaded equations. Blue dashed lines show the antibunching asymptotes, and green dashed lines show the coherent-filtering limit. The normalized amplitude is 𝑆𝑛𝛿𝜔 pr = ⟨𝜎pr −⟩𝑛𝛿𝜔/ ⟨𝜎pr −⟩coh 𝑛𝛿𝜔is the 𝛾s →∞value.

Figure 3 shows the crossover between the two regimes. For small 𝛾s/𝛾pr, the +3𝛿𝜔and −5𝛿𝜔peaks follow the linear laws (47) and (48), while the +5𝛿𝜔peak follows the quadratic law (49). For large 𝛾s/𝛾pr, all three normalized amplitudes saturate to unity, as expected in the coherent-filtering regime. The numerical results therefore confirm both the analytical side-peak formulas and the physical interpretation in terms of antibunching-induced suppression of multiphoton source pathways.
